title: Tailwind CSS Best Practices
intro: Tailwind CSS lets you style components with utility classes directly in your markup. It's like having a spice rack instead of pre-mixed seasoning packets — you pick exactly what you need for each dish. Judge whether each Tailwind practice is good or bad.

teach.title: Why Tailwind?
teach.body: Tailwind CSS uses small, single-purpose utility classes like `p-4` (padding), `text-lg` (font size), and `bg-blue-500` (background color). Instead of writing CSS in separate files and inventing class names, you compose styles directly in your JSX. This keeps styles co-located with the markup they affect and eliminates the problem of dead CSS.

teach.title: Speed Dial on Your Phone
teach.body: Typing a full phone number every time you call someone would be tedious. That's why you save contacts — one tap replaces ten digits. Extracting a React component from a long Tailwind class list works the same way: instead of repeating `rounded-lg shadow-md p-4 bg-white` in five places, you create a `<Card>` component and use that single name everywhere. The long class string is typed once, then reused with a short name.

teach.title: Taming Long Class Lists
teach.body: The most common complaint about Tailwind is long class strings. The solution isn't to avoid Tailwind — it's to **extract components**. If a class list appears in multiple places, that's a sign you need a React component, not a custom CSS class. The component becomes your reusable abstraction.

teach.title: Tailwind Is Like a Spice Rack
teach.body: Traditional CSS is like cooking with pre-mixed seasoning packets — convenient but inflexible. Tailwind is like having a full spice rack where you pick exactly the spices you need for each dish. `p-4` is your salt, `rounded-lg` is your pepper, `bg-blue-500` is your paprika. You combine them fresh every time, and the result is always exactly what you want.

Every polished SaaS product that makes money has a consistent, professional-looking UI. Mastering Tailwind means you can build production-quality interfaces fast — and ship apps that customers actually pay for.

Tailwind works best when paired with component-driven design. Use utility classes for styling, extract React components when you see duplication, and lean on Tailwind's design system (spacing scale, color palette) to keep your UI consistent without manual effort.

cards.content: Organizing Tailwind classes in a consistent order: layout (flex, grid) first, then spacing (p, m), then typography (text, font), then colors (bg, text), then effects (shadow, rounded)
cards.explanation: Consistent class ordering makes long class strings scannable. You always know where to look for a specific type of style. Many teams adopt a standard order and enforce it with tooling like prettier-plugin-tailwindcss.

cards.content: Using arbitrary values everywhere like p-[13px], mt-[7px], and text-[15px] instead of Tailwind's built-in spacing and size scales
cards.explanation: Arbitrary values bypass Tailwind's design system, leading to inconsistent spacing and sizing across your app. Stick to the built-in scale (p-3, mt-2, text-sm) so everything aligns to a consistent rhythm.

cards.content: Using responsive prefixes like 'grid grid-cols-1 md:grid-cols-2 lg:grid-cols-3' to make the task list adapt from mobile to desktop
cards.explanation: Tailwind's responsive prefixes make mobile-first design straightforward. The base style applies to mobile, then md: and lg: add overrides for larger screens. No media queries needed.

cards.content: Adding the ! prefix to force overrides like '!text-red-500 !font-bold' because your styles keep getting overridden by other classes
cards.explanation: If you need !important to make styles work, your component structure has a problem. Fix the root cause (conflicting styles, wrong specificity) instead of forcing overrides. Using ! makes styles fragile and hard to debug.

cards.content: Using Tailwind's dark: variant like 'bg-white dark:bg-gray-900 text-gray-900 dark:text-white' to support both light and dark themes
cards.explanation: The dark: variant is the idiomatic way to handle dark mode in Tailwind. It keeps both themes visible in one place and works with Tailwind's built-in dark mode detection.

cards.content: Mixing Tailwind classes with inline styles like <div className='flex' style={{ marginTop: '20px', color: '#333' }}>
cards.explanation: Mixing inline styles with Tailwind defeats the purpose of a utility system. Use mt-5 and text-gray-700 instead. Inline styles can't use responsive prefixes, dark mode, or hover states.

cards.content: When the same combination of 'rounded-lg shadow-md p-4 bg-white border border-gray-200' appears on 5 different cards, extracting a reusable <Card> React component
cards.explanation: This is exactly the right move. Don't use @apply to create a CSS abstraction — create a React component instead. The component is your reusable unit, and it can carry behavior (onClick, children) alongside its styles.
